EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with a strong low level jet and a near tropical atmosphere to produce a severe MCS across southern Minnesota.  The MCS was in its decaying stage by the time it reached northeast Iowa and northern Illinois.  However, a few storms managed to produce localized downbursts that downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and limbs down across the city.  One tree fell on a house causing minor damage.  Several trees were downed in Miller-Riverview Park and the arboretum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary extending from northwest Minnesota into southwest Kansas advanced toward Iowa.  The flow was very week, but the airmass was very moist.  Precipitable water values were above 2 inches with a deep warm layer in place.  The freezing level was between 14,500 and 15,500 feet.  The CAPE in the -10 to -30 layer was significant at 800 J/kg, but in spite of that there was little hail due to the very high freezing level.  Afternoon temperatures warmed in to the upper 80s to low 90s with dew points in the mid 70s.  This pushed the most unstable CAPE to around 5500 J/kg.  Lifted indices were in the -9 to -11 range by late afternoon.  Tornadoes did not occur with a weak shear environment (under 20 kts) and with an LCL of 1000 to 1200 meters.  There was a fair amount of DCAPE available, about 1500 J/kg.  Thunderstorms formed in three areas as the afternoon progressed.  One area was in northwest Iowa, with the second over eastern Nebraska moving into west central and southwest Iowa.  The third area was smaller with development over the southeast counties of the state.  Initially the storms produced some marginally severe half of three quarter to one inch in diameter.  As the afternoon progressed, the two clusters in northwest and southwest Iowa merged, and the storms over the southeast weakened.  The systems over the west took on more of a linear structure and the event transitioned into a wind event.  There were several reports of trees and power lines downed by the high winds.  The strongest winds was measured at 75 MPH in Adair.

An upper level disturbance in a near tropical atmosphere produced scattered thunderstorms in the pre-dawn hours across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois.  Hail was nearly non existent or was of pea sized due to the high freezing levels.  Several of the storms pulsed severe with high winds that downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fascia off a building with bricks laying all over with a power feeder line on the ground

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The frontal boundary that had been across Iowa the past few days remained stationary across the state during the afternoon.  Two other weather features interacted with the airmass during the late afternoon hours.  One was a mesoscale convective vorticity maximum that moved from southern Nebraska, east toward Iowa.  A strong upper level trough of low pressure dropped southeast out of Canada, along with its associated cold frontal boundary, into Iowa late in the day.  The airmass became very unstable by late afternoon with high temperatures in the mid to upper 80s and dew point temperatures in the upper 60s to low 70s.  Lifted indices dropped to the -7 to -12 C. range with CAPE values in the 3500 to 5500 J/kg range.  The freezing level was quite high int he 13,800 to 14,600 foot range, limiting the hail size in spite of the CAPE of 300 to 500 J/kg in the -10 C. to -30 C. layer.  The tornado theat was limited by the LCL height being in the 800 to 1600 meter range.  Thunderstorms developed in western Iowa initially with a few severe storms reported.  Some of the initial thunderstorms produced high winds with 20 inch diameter tree branches broken off in Humboldt County at Livermore.  As the late afternoon and evening progressed, a line of thunderstorms formed along the cold frontal boundary.  Shear in the 30 to 40 kt range helped maintain the storms.  There were reports of quarter size hail from some of the cells.  Wind became more of an issue later in the event.  Along the line that formed along the northern cold front, winds gusted to 61 MPH in the Fort Dodge area and winds near 60 MPH west of town.  The winds caused an accident on U.S. Highway 20 near Moorland.  As the storms progressed southeast, strong winds moved through the Des Moines metro area.  Railroad crossing arms were broken by the high wind in Des Moines.  Farther southeast, near Hartford, wind gusts were estimated around 65 MPH by an off duty NWS employee.

Scattered thunderstorms developed in a moist atmosphere ahead of a cold front moving through eastern Iowa.  Remnant outflow boundaries from convection earlier in the day allowed the storms to pulse severe with the primary threat being large hail.  Boundary interactions with the storms resulted in numerous reports of funnel clouds from the public.  One boundary interaction resulted in a landspout a few miles north of Keota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: chunks the size of golfballs and possibly larger

A thunderstorm interacted with an outflow boundary from convection earlier in the day and produced a funnel that was visible for several minutes prior to touching down.  Based on available information, the landspout touched down just west of the intersection of 180th street and W15 (30th Ave) and moved south southeast.  The landspout partially damaged a field of soy beans before reaching the Becker Farm.  A garage was destroyed with windows broken out of the farm house.  Several 18 inch diameter trees were snapped off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: landspout; rated F1 with winds estimated around 90 mph

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system lifted northeast into the central U.S. out of the Monsoonal flow in the southwest U.S.  The low was slow moving as it advanced across the area.  A considerable amount of moisture was drawn north ahead of the system with precipitable water values in the 1.75 to 2.25 inch range.  As the low advanced northeast, a large area of rain and embedded thunderstorms developed.  The system took on characteristics of a cool season system with a large deformation zone forming along the north edge of the system.  With the deep moisture in place, copious amounts of rain fell in a narrow band, about 30 miles wide, extended east of Omaha to north of Waterloo.  Rainfall amounts of 2 to 5 inches were common in this area.  The heaviest rain fell north of Des Moines at Popejoy in Franklin County.  Official rainfall totals from the area were around 8 inches, with some unofficial amounts as high as a little over 9.5 inches.   Radar estimates show that between 3 and 5 inches of the rain fell in the hour between 0500 and 0600 CST.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding southeast of Popejoy with several county roads being closed by high water flowing over the road.

One area was in northwest Iowa, with the second over eastern Nebraska moving into west central and southwest Iowa.  The third area was smaller with development over the southeast counties of the state.  Initially the storms produced some marginally severe half of three quarter to one inch in diameter.  As the afternoon progressed, the two clusters in northwest and southwest Iowa merged, and the storms over the southeast weakened.  The systems over the west took on more of a linear structure and the event transitioned into a wind event.  There were several reports of trees and power lines downed by the high winds.  The strongest winds was measured at 75 MPH in Adair.

An upper level disturbance in a near tropical atmosphere produced scattered thunderstorms in the pre-dawn hours across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois.  Hail was nearly non existent or was of pea sized due to the high freezing levels.  Several of the storms pulsed severe with high winds that downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: 10 inch diameter tree snapped and several 8-9 inch diameter pine trees topped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A likely upper level disturbance initiated scattered thunderstorms in a semi-dry to semi-moist atmosphere across eastern Iowa.  Two storms pulsed severe and produced one report each of large hail and a downburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance in a near tropical atmosphere produced scattered thunderstorms in the pre-dawn hours across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois.  Hail was nearly non existent or was of pea sized due to the high freezing levels.  Several of the storms pulsed severe with high winds that downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 3 foot diameter cottonwood limb was torn off its parent tree and dropped on a nearby house.  Extensive damage was done to the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped southeast across the state during the day on the 1st into the evening hours.  By midnight...the front extended east-northeast to west-southwest across southern Iowa.  The soundings indicated a considerable amount of dry air in the low levels, thus preventing the formation of tornadoes.  The LCL was in the 5500 to 6000 foot range during the event.  High temperatures warmed into the upper 80s over far northwest Iowa, to the 95 to 100 degree range over the south.  Dew points were generally in the upper 50s to mid 60s except along the frontal boundary.  Moisture pooled along the boundary with dew points rising into the low 70s.  The atmosphere became quiet unstable with lifted indices around -9 C. and CAPE values in the 3500 to 4500 J/kg range.  There was some shear available, but it wasn't all that strong.  Generally shear in the zero to 3 km layer was in the 25 to 30 kt range.  The freezing level was quite high, being in the 14,500 to 15,000 foot range.  Thunderstorms fired initially just to the north of the boundary in northwest and north central Iowa, along the north edge of the mid level cap.  The storm development was enhanced as a jet speed max raced across southern North Dakota into Wisconsin during the late afternoon, putting Iowa in the 3rd quadrant, or right entrance region, of the jet.  These storms produced three quarter to one inch diameter hail in a few areas.  The largest hail occurred in the Clear Lake area and was the size of half dollars.  Gusty winds were also reported, however most were in the 35 to 50 MPH range.  One wind gust to around 60 MPH downed tree limbs in Humboldt County at Bradgate.  As the evening progressed, an outflow boundary from the convection in the north pushed south into central Iowa.  Scattered thunderstorms developed along the boundary, but most were not severe.  One storm produced a 62 MPH wind gust as it passed over Grimes in Polk County.  The storm had formed along the outflow boundary, but weakened quickly as the outflow boundary undercut the storm.  Thunderstorms continued to fire during the night to the north of the actual frontal boundary.  These were quite elevated.  One of the storms became severe briefly and dropped one inch diameter hail southeast of Boxholm in Boone County.

The frontal boundary that had been across Iowa the past few days remained stationary across the state during the afternoon.  Two other weather features interacted with the airmass during the late afternoon hours.  One was a mesoscale convective vorticity maximum that moved from southern Nebraska, east toward Iowa.  A strong upper level trough of low pressure dropped southeast out of Canada, along with its associated cold frontal boundary, into Iowa late in the day.  The airmass became very unstable by late afternoon with high temperatures in the mid to upper 80s and dew point temperatures in the upper 60s to low 70s.  Lifted indices dropped to the -7 to -12 C. range with CAPE values in the 3500 to 5500 J/kg range.  The freezing level was quite high int he 13,800 to 14,600 foot range, limiting the hail size in spite of the CAPE of 300 to 500 J/kg in the -10 C. to -30 C. layer.  The tornado theat was limited by the LCL height being in the 800 to 1600 meter range.  Thunderstorms developed in western Iowa initially with a few severe storms reported.  Some of the initial thunderstorms produced high winds with 20 inch diameter tree branches broken off in Humboldt County at Livermore.  As the late afternoon and evening progressed, a line of thunderstorms formed along the cold frontal boundary.  Shear in the 30 to 40 kt range helped maintain the storms.  There were reports of quarter size hail from some of the cells.  Wind became more of an issue later in the event.  Along the line that formed along the northern cold front, winds gusted to 61 MPH in the Fort Dodge area and winds near 60 MPH west of town.  The winds caused an accident on U.S. Highway 20 near Moorland.  As the storms progressed southeast, strong winds moved through the Des Moines metro area.  Railroad crossing arms were broken by the high wind in Des Moines.  Farther southeast, near Hartford, wind gusts were estimated around 65 MPH by an off duty NWS employee.

Scattered thunderstorms developed in a moist atmosphere ahead of a cold front moving through eastern Iowa.  Remnant outflow boundaries from convection earlier in the day allowed the storms to pulse severe with the primary threat being large hail.  Boundary interactions with the storms resulted in numerous reports of funnel clouds from the public.  One boundary interaction resulted in a landspout a few miles north of Keota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: estimated 0.50 to 1 inch of rain in 30 minutes with minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance initiated thunderstorms in a nearly moist atmosphere ahead of a stationary front running from northwest Missouri into northeast Illinois.  Numerous storms pulsed severe across southeast Iowa and west central Illinois with wind being the primary threat.  Once the storms moved far enough away from the front and into drier air, hail and wind became a combined threat across west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: 4 inch diameter branch down on fence.

There were reports of quarter size hail from some of the cells.  Wind became more of an issue later in the event.  Along the line that formed along the northern cold front, winds gusted to 61 MPH in the Fort Dodge area and winds near 60 MPH west of town.  The winds caused an accident on U.S. Highway 20 near Moorland.  As the storms progressed southeast, strong winds moved through the Des Moines metro area.  Railroad crossing arms were broken by the high wind in Des Moines.  Farther southeast, near Hartford, wind gusts were estimated around 65 MPH by an off duty NWS employee.

Scattered thunderstorms developed in a moist atmosphere ahead of a cold front moving through eastern Iowa.  Remnant outflow boundaries from convection earlier in the day allowed the storms to pulse severe with the primary threat being large hail.  Boundary interactions with the storms resulted in numerous reports of funnel clouds from the public.  One boundary interaction resulted in a landspout a few miles north of Keota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: near the golf course

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance initiated two supercell thunderstorms in a moist to near tropical atmosphere that was ahead of a cold front in eastern Iowa.  Outflow from the supercells developed a broken line of of storms that moved through Iowa and into Illinois and Missouri.  The line produced sporatic pulse severe storms from east central Iowa into northwest Illinois.  The more solid part of the line produced a considerable amount of severe weather from east central Iowa into northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: 1.5 inches of rain in the past hour

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across north central Iowa along an elevated convergence zone in a near tropical atmosphere.  Feed by a low level jet of 30 knots (about 35 mph), the thunderstorms evolved into a squall line that raced east southeast across eastern Iowa and northern Illinois.  Wind damage was the most common severe report with severe crop damage occurring across a large portion of Benton County, and to a lesser extent across Iowa, Linn, and Clinton Counties.  The wind combined with a large amount of cloud to ground lightning resulted in power failures across parts of eastern Iowa and took two public safety communication towers off the air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a 7 inch diameter walnut limb down and a 9 inch diameter oak limb down.  Heavy rain of 1.10 inches was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately unstable airmass was in place across Iowa ahead of an approaching cold front.  The airmass was tropical in nature with a clear tropical connection evident on satellite pictures.  Precipitable water values were in the 2 to 2.5 inch range by mid afternoon.  Considerable debris cloudiness was over much of the state during the day from convection the previous night to the west.  The clouds kept temperatures from warming much above the 85 to 90 F. degree range ahead of the cold front, though dew points were in the mid 70s throughout the area.  Lifted indices were around -3 C.  Moderate CAPE was available and was in the 1500 to 2500 J/kg range.  The environment was weakly sheared with 20 to 30 kts of shear.  Thunderstorms persisted through the day over northwest Iowa ahead of the front.  During the afternoon, additional thunderstorms formed just ahead of the persistent area.  There was one report of golf ball size hail in Davis County, but hail was limited given the freezing level of 16,400 feet.  A few of the storms produced strong winds with a 60 MPH wind reported in Davis County.  The roof was damaged on a house south of Pulaski.  Storms downed several outbuildings and flattened a corn field west and north of Lacey  in Mahaska County.  That same storms also dropped 3 inches of rain in the area.  The main feature was the very heavy rainfall.  Thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall in the Osceola area of Clarke County.  Reports indicated 4.78 inches fell a half mile north of Osceola in under 2 hours.  A young boy and girl were playing in water after the rain near Murray.  The little girl went under and several feet underground through a pipe and ended up in a drainage ditch.  Her brother followed and suffered a broken leg.  Heavy rainfall also occurred in the Ottumwa area with nearly 2 inches falling in about an hour there as well.  Lightning struck a house northwest of Drakesville, in Davis County, setting it on fire.  The house was a total loss.  Another storm caused a fire near New Sharon in Mahaska County.  Lightning struck the barn, setting the wiring on fire.  Damage was limited to the roof area for the  most part.  The same storm produced high winds that blew over two sheds and a garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and very moist airmass was in place over Iowa.  The airmass was actually very tropical in nature with warm cloudy depths of nearly 4.5 km and precipitable water values around 50 mm (2 inches).  Weak surface low pressure moved east across northern MO as an upper level low pressure developed over Iowa.  Heavy rainfall occurred with these features with several areas of the state picking up fro 1 to 4 inches of rain.  Heavier rain fell over Taylor County with the heaviest rain falling between 2130 CST on the 27th and 0100 CDT on the 28th.  Much of county received 2 to 3 inches of rain during that period of time.  An unofficial report was received about 3 miles southeast of Bedford of 9.7 inches.  Flooding became serious with numerous county roads flooded and several washouts reported.  Flooding was serious along the Honey Creek the East Fork 102 River.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused flooding of roads and low areas.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including branches blown down.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree and power line damage.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree and power line damage, resulting in a power outage.

Thunderstorm winds caused tree and power line damage, resulting in power outages. Tree debris block several streets. The power outages affected Battle creek and nearby areas, with the outages lasting from 2 to 6 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic situation set up for the afternoon of the 16th into the early morning hours of the 17th.  An upper level low pressure area lifted northeast across the northern Plains.  A strong upper level jet lifted northeast on the east side of the upper low with wind speeds in the jet in the 80 to 100 kt range.  Meanwhile, a surface cold front approached Iowa from the northwest.  The airmass became quite unstable in front of the cold front as a broad fetch of warm and moist air overspread the state.  High temperatures reached the mid 80s to mid 90s, with dew point readings in the mid 60s to low 70s.  By evening, the lifted index values were in the -3 to -6 C. range with 2000 to 3000 J/kg of CAPE.  Plenty of moisture was available with precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 2 inch range.  The effective shear in the zero to 3 km range was near 60 kts.  Thunderstorms fired very late in the afternoon to the west of the CWA and advanced into Iowa.  Initially, the storms were somewhat discreet, though severe weather remained limited.  Parameters were favorable with the LCL in the 800 to 1200 meter range, CAPE in the -10 to -30 C. layer was around 400 J/kg, and the downdraft CAPE was in the 1000 to 1500 J/kg range.  Large hail was not realized, with spotty reports of hail to nickel size received.  One of the main reasons for the lack of large hail reports is the fact that the freezing level just ahead of the line was 16,800 feet.  As the evening progressed, the storms became more linear and began to produce high winds.  There were numerous reports of winds in excess of 50 MPH all along the line.  In addition several locations reported severe wind speeds in the 60 to 70 MPH range.  The strongest wind occurred in the Fontanelle area of Adair County with a wind gust of 76 MPH.   High winds north of Fontanelle downed 6 power poles and severely damaged the front porch of a house and damaged 2 barns.   For the most part, damage was limited to trees and spotty crop damage.  Thunderstorms trained over parts of western Iowa.  In Cass County, thunderstorms produced from 2 to 4 inches of rain in the Atlantic area, causing urban flooding between 2300 on the 16th and 0100 on the 17th.  Water shot into the air 2 to 3 feet from the storm sewers and one street buckled due to the water pressure.  Damage was around $50,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer estimated thunderstorm winds gusted to 60 mph near Little Sioux.

High winds north of Fontanelle downed 6 power poles and severely damaged the front porch of a house and damaged 2 barns.   For the most part, damage was limited to trees and spotty crop damage.  Thunderstorms trained over parts of western Iowa.  In Cass County, thunderstorms produced from 2 to 4 inches of rain in the Atlantic area, causing urban flooding between 2300 on the 16th and 0100 on the 17th.  Water shot into the air 2 to 3 feet from the storm sewers and one street buckled due to the water pressure.  Damage was around $50,000.

A very dynamic situation set up for the afternoon of the 16th into the early morning hours of the 17th.  An upper level low pressure area lifted northeast across the northern Plains.  A strong upper level jet lifted northeast on the east side of the upper low with wind speeds in the jet in the 80 to 100 kt range.  Meanwhile, a surface cold front approached Iowa from the northwest.  The airmass became quite unstable in front of the cold front as a broad fetch of warm and moist air overspread the state.  High temperatures reached the mid 80s to mid 90s, with dew point readings in the mid 60s to low 70s.  By evening, the lifted index values were in the -3 to -6 C. range with 2000 to 3000 J/kg of CAPE.  Plenty of moisture was available with precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 2 inch range.  The effective shear in the zero to 3 km range was near 60 kts.  Thunderstorms fired very late in the afternoon to the west of the CWA and advanced into Iowa.  Initially, the storms were somewhat discreet, though severe weather remained limited.  Parameters were favorable with the LCL in the 800 to 1200 meter range, CAPE in the -10 to -30 C. layer was around 400 J/kg, and the downdraft CAPE was in the 1000 to 1500 J/kg range.  Large hail was not realized, with spotty reports of hail to nickel size received.  One of the main reasons for the lack of large hail reports is the fact that the freezing level just ahead of the line was 16,800 feet. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including numerous large trees blown down.
Large hail covered the ground.
Large hail covered the ground up to 4 inches deep. Some hail was still on the ground over 2 hours after it stopped falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front advanced into Iowa in advance of a strong upper air low pressure that was over central South Dakota.  Instability was marginal with CAPE values in the 1000 J/kg range and lifted indices in the -2 to -4 C. range.  A weak shear environment was in place with 20 kts or less of shear in the 0-6 km layer.  Much of the CAPE that was available was in the lowest levels of the atmosphere.  Thunderstorms erupted along the initial surface boundary during peak heating.  With LCL in the 600-1000 meter range, combined with the amount of low level CAPE, the thunderstorms produced numerous funnel clouds.  There were three reported touchdowns.  They were actually more of a landspout that a full fledged tornado.  Little damage occurred as they touched down briefly in open farm files.  The thunderstorms also produced some localized very heavy rainfall.  Satellite pictures shows evidence of a strong tropical connection to Hurricane John in the east Pacific.  Local rainfall of 3 to 4.5 inches in about an hour was reported on the west side of the Des Moines metropolitan area.  There were several reports of 1 to 3 inch rainfall along the Interstate 35 corridor.  Some local street flooding was reported through this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado touchdown in open fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped south out of southern Minnesota during the late afternoon and early evening hours.  The dynamic situation was weak with lifted indices in the -4 to -5 C. range and only about 1100 J/kg of CAPE.  The shear environment was quite weak as well with only 20 to 25 kts of shear.  Being as it was into September, freezing levels were relatively low for the thunderstorms, making it fairly easy to get hail out of them.  The freezing level was around 12,000 to 12,500 feet.  A small area of thunderstorms formed over north central Iowa.  They produced three quarter to one inch diameter hail.  One of the storms produced high winds in Wright County.  Large tree limbs were broken off and one large tree was snapped off.

A cold front dropped southeast through Iowa during the day on the 9th and became stationary south of the state.  During the night of the 9th into the early morning of the 10th, low pressure formed over Oklahoma.  The low lifted northeast along the front during the day on the 10th.  A large area of over running precipitation developed.  The airmass became very unstable with a southerly jet of 35 to 40 kts lifting over the cooler air at the surface.  Dew points at the 850 mb level were near +10 C. in the warm air.  Considerable destabilization took place with elevated CAPE rising into the 3000 to 4000 J/kg range over the east half of Iowa.  The freezing level was around 10,000 feet, while the available cape in the -10 to -30 C. layer was around 200 J/kg.  Thunderstorms erupted over a large part of Iowa, with a line of stronger storms developing in the south central into parts of central Iowa.  Thunderstorms produced nickel size hail in Jasper County, which covered the ground.  Penny size hail covered the ground in Poweshiek County from another severe storm.  To the northwest, snow, mixed with freezing rain and sleet at times, was the problem.  The snow began accumulating around 0400 CST, with the heavier snow beginning around 0600 CST.  Heavy snow fell over the northern two tiers of counties, with 6 to 10 inches common.  The heaviest snow fell in a band from Palo Alto County, across Kossuth County, into Winnebago County with 8 to 10 inches common.  In Cerro Gordo County, snowfall was lighter with 3 to 4 inches common.  In addition to the snow, heavy sleet fell at the onset of of the storm.  Reports of around an inch of sleet were received.  The snowfall tapered off rapidly too the south of the heavy snow area with amounts south of U.S. Highway 20, south to Interstate 80, generally in the 1 to 3 inch range.

Water shot into the air 2 to 3 feet from the storm sewers and one street buckled due to the water pressure.  Damage was around $50,000.

A very dynamic situation set up for the afternoon of the 16th into the early morning hours of the 17th.  An upper level low pressure area lifted northeast across the northern Plains.  A strong upper level jet lifted northeast on the east side of the upper low with wind speeds in the jet in the 80 to 100 kt range.  Meanwhile, a surface cold front approached Iowa from the northwest.  The airmass became quite unstable in front of the cold front as a broad fetch of warm and moist air overspread the state.  High temperatures reached the mid 80s to mid 90s, with dew point readings in the mid 60s to low 70s.  By evening, the lifted index values were in the -3 to -6 C. range with 2000 to 3000 J/kg of CAPE.  Plenty of moisture was available with precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 2 inch range.  The effective shear in the zero to 3 km range was near 60 kts.  Thunderstorms fired very late in the afternoon to the west of the CWA and advanced into Iowa.  Initially, the storms were somewhat discreet, though severe weather remained limited.  Parameters were favorable with the LCL in the 800 to 1200 meter range, CAPE in the -10 to -30 C. layer was around 400 J/kg, and the downdraft CAPE was in the 1000 to 1500 J/kg range.  Large hail was not realized, with spotty reports of hail to nickel size received.  One of the main reasons for the lack of large hail reports is the fact that the freezing level just ahead of the line was 16,800 feet.  As the evening progressed, the storms became more linear and began to produce high winds.  There were numerous reports of winds in excess of 50 MPH all along the line.  In addition several locations reported severe wind speeds in the 60 to 70 MPH range.  The strongest wind occurred in the Fontanelle area of Adair County with a wind gust of 76 MPH.   High winds north of Fontanelle downed 6 power poles and severely damaged the front porch of a house and damaged 2 barns.   For the most part, damage was limited to trees and spotty crop damage.  Thunderstorms trained over parts of western Iowa.  In Cass County, thunderstorms produced from 2 to 4 inches of rain in the Atlantic area, causing urban flooding between 2300 on the 16th and 0100 on the 17th.  Water shot into the air 2 to 3 feet from the storm sewers and one street buckled due to the water pressure.  Damage was around $50,000.

A cold front advanced into Iowa in advance of a strong upper air low pressure that was over central South Dakota.  Instability was marginal with CAPE values in the 1000 J/kg range and lifted indices in the -2 to -4 C. range.  A weak shear environment was in place with 20 kts or less of shear in the 0-6 km layer.  Much of the CAPE that was available was in the lowest levels of the atmosphere.  Thunderstorms erupted along the initial surface boundary during peak heating.  With LCL in the 600-1000 meter range, combined with the amount of low level CAPE, the thunderstorms produced numerous funnel clouds.  There were three reported touchdowns.  They were actually more of a landspout that a full fledged tornado.  Little damage occurred as they touched down briefly in open farm files.  The thunderstorms also produced some localized very heavy rainfall.  Satellite pictures shows evidence of a strong tropical connection to Hurricane John in the east Pacific.  Local rainfall of 3 to 4.5 inches in about an hour was reported on the west side of the Des Moines metropolitan area.  There were several reports of 1 to 3 inch rainfall along the Interstate 35 corridor.  Some local street flooding was reported through this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado touchdown in open fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped south out of southern Minnesota during the late afternoon and early evening hours.  The dynamic situation was weak with lifted indices in the -4 to -5 C. range and only about 1100 J/kg of CAPE.  The shear environment was quite weak as well with only 20 to 25 kts of shear.  Being as it was into September, freezing levels were relatively low for the thunderstorms, making it fairly easy to get hail out of them.  The freezing level was around 12,000 to 12,500 feet.  A small area of thunderstorms formed over north central Iowa.  They produced three quarter to one inch diameter hail.  One of the storms produced high winds in Wright County.  Large tree limbs were broken off and one large tree was snapped off.

An area of thunderstorms lifted northeast across Iowa in an area of strong warm air advection.  A low level jet of 55 kts was in place to keep the thunderstorms going.  Zero to 6 km shear was around 35 kts with CAPE in the 1000 to 1500 range.  Most of the storms produced gusty winds and small hail.  One of the storms produced a 65 MPH wind gust as it moved through the Williams area.

Moderate to heavy rains helped recharge several watersheds in central and southern Iowa, and there were some areas that had elevated flow due to the rains.  Along the Raccoon Basin, heavy rains brought some bankfull conditions at mid month, however no flooding was recorded.  Along the Skunk Basin in central Iowa, some minor flooding would result at Ames from some significant upstream rainfall on September 11th, with river levels rapidly returning to normal following the event.  In addition to the flooding along the Skunk River, 4 to 6 inches of rain in Boone County resulted in some minor flooding around the county as small streams overtopped their banks.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local media report indicated estimated thunderstorm winds of 60 mph near Underwood Iowa.
Emergency management estimated thunderstorm winds gusted to 60 mph.

A weak trough moved east across Iowa during the afternoon and evening of the 29th.  A few thunderstorms formed along the upper level vorticity center.  The atmosphere was not all that unstable with little CAPE and a nearly neutral lifted index, however the soundings did present a good straight line pattern with an inverted "V" sounding.  As the thunderstorms moved through the Fort Dodge area they produced high winds.  Several trees were downed as well as power line damage reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including a large tree blown down across a road.
Thunderstorm winds blew down power lines. The amount of damage was not known.
Thunderstorm winds uprooted a large tree and caused other tree damage, including several large limbs blown down.

As the evening progressed, the storms became more linear and began to produce high winds.  There were numerous reports of winds in excess of 50 MPH all along the line.  In addition several locations reported severe wind speeds in the 60 to 70 MPH range.  The strongest wind occurred in the Fontanelle area of Adair County with a wind gust of 76 MPH.   High winds north of Fontanelle downed 6 power poles and severely damaged the front porch of a house and damaged 2 barns.   For the most part, damage was limited to trees and spotty crop damage.  Thunderstorms trained over parts of western Iowa.  In Cass County, thunderstorms produced from 2 to 4 inches of rain in the Atlantic area, causing urban flooding between 2300 on the 16th and 0100 on the 17th.  Water shot into the air 2 to 3 feet from the storm sewers and one street buckled due to the water pressure.  Damage was around $50,000.

A very dynamic situation set up for the afternoon of the 16th into the early morning hours of the 17th.  An upper level low pressure area lifted northeast across the northern Plains.  A strong upper level jet lifted northeast on the east side of the upper low with wind speeds in the jet in the 80 to 100 kt range.  Meanwhile, a surface cold front approached Iowa from the northwest.  The airmass became quite unstable in front of the cold front as a broad fetch of warm and moist air overspread the state.  High temperatures reached the mid 80s to mid 90s, with dew point readings in the mid 60s to low 70s.  By evening, the lifted index values were in the -3 to -6 C. range with 2000 to 3000 J/kg of CAPE.  Plenty of moisture was available with precipitable water values in the 1.5 to 2 inch range.  The effective shear in the zero to 3 km range was near 60 kts.  Thunderstorms fired very late in the afternoon to the west of the CWA and advanced into Iowa.  Initially, the storms were somewhat discreet, though severe weather remained limited.  Parameters were favorable with the LCL in the 800 to 1200 meter range, CAPE in the -10 to -30 C. layer was around 400 J/kg, and the downdraft CAPE was in the 1000 to 1500 J/kg range. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported by the public.
Law enforcement officials reported funnel clouds, none of which touched down, across northwest portions of Winneshiek County.

Scattered thunderstorms developed across north central Iowa along an elevated convergence zone in a near tropical atmosphere.  Feed by a low level jet of 30 knots (about 35 mph), the thunderstorms evolved into a squall line that raced east southeast across eastern Iowa and northern Illinois.  Wind damage was the most common severe report with severe crop damage occurring across a large portion of Benton County, and to a lesser extent across Iowa, Linn, and Clinton Counties.  The wind combined with a large amount of cloud to ground lightning resulted in power failures across parts of eastern Iowa and took two public safety communication towers off the air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A probable lightning strike took down a tree in Vinton.

Thunderstorm winds damaged crops and blew down branches. The amount of crop or tree damage was not known.
Thunderstorm winds estimated at 60 mph caused scattered tree damage around town, including 1 tree limb that fell onto a law enforcement vehicle causing significant damage.
A large tree was downed south of Blencoe and corn was flattened in the area by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance initiated two supercell thunderstorms in a moist to near tropical atmosphere that was ahead of a cold front in eastern Iowa.  Outflow from the supercells developed a broken line of of storms that moved through Iowa and into Illinois and Missouri.  The line produced sporatic pulse severe storms from east central Iowa into northwest Illinois.  The more solid part of the line produced a considerable amount of severe weather from east central Iowa into northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees & limbs down; roof partially off a business

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with a low level jet to initiate a squall line in a near tropical atmosphere across western and central Iowa.  The squall line moved quickly into southeast Iowa and northern Missouri before dissipating.  Numerous reports of pea size hail and non severe winds of 39 to 43 knots (45 to 50 mph) were received.  The severe events were sporatic in nature and were mainly wind from likely downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported at an insurance office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance initiated an MCS across Minnesota in a semi-moist atmosphere that moved east southeast into southern Wisconsin before decaying.  A lone storm pulsed severe on an outflow boundary from the MCS and produced penny to nickel hail in Jo Daviess and Stephenson Counties in Illinois.  The same outflow boundary aided in the non severe thunderstorm development in the Quad Cities which resulted in locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass was in place over Iowa during the afternoon of the 9th into the overnight hours of the 10th.  A warm front at thesurface was located just to the south of Iowa.  Meanwhile, the warm front aloft was located well to the north of the surface front.  Moisture pooled to the north of the surface front with surface dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s through much of the evening and early morning hours.  Precipitable water values were in the 1.75 to 2 inch range.  Strong theta-e advection took place during the evening and overnight hours with a west-southwesterly low level jet of about 30 kts.  CAPE values were in the 4000 to 5500 J/kg range through much of the night with lifted indices in the -11 C. range.  The shear environment was favorable with 35 to 45 kts of effective shear in place in the zero to 3 km layer.  LCL values were in the 600 to 1000 meter range.  There was plenty of CAPE available in the -10 to -30 C. layer with 500 to 700 J/kg available.  In spite of this, little hail occurred with the freezing level quite high, nearly 15,000 feet.   Downdraft CAPE was around 1500 J/kg, favoring winds.  Thunderstorms erupted north of the warm front.  They formed in two areas.  An MCS formed over eastern Nebraska into western Iowa, north of the warm front and just ahead of a surface low pressure area that was located over northeast Kansas.  The second area formed over central in to east central Iowa.  The principle mode of severe weather was wind, though not as widespread as may have been expected.  Scattered reports of winds of 60 to 70 MPH were reported.  The winds caused considerable tree and power line damage, but damage was not widespread.

An upper level disturbance combined with outflow boundaries from early morning convection to initiate thunderstorms in a near tropical atmosphere.  Two storms pulsed severe and produced a couple of severe wind reports and marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: dime size hail

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with outflow boundaries from early morning convection to initiate thunderstorms in a near tropical atmosphere.  Two storms pulsed severe and produced a couple of severe wind reports and marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: corn flattened with pea size hail on the Bryan Lasek farm

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass was in place over Iowa during the afternoon of the 9th into the overnight hours of the 10th.  A warm front at thesurface was located just to the south of Iowa.  Meanwhile, the warm front aloft was located well to the north of the surface front.  Moisture pooled to the north of the surface front with surface dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s through much of the evening and early morning hours.  Precipitable water values were in the 1.75 to 2 inch range.  Strong theta-e advection took place during the evening and overnight hours with a west-southwesterly low level jet of about 30 kts.  CAPE values were in the 4000 to 5500 J/kg range through much of the night with lifted indices in the -11 C. range.  The shear environment was favorable with 35 to 45 kts of effective shear in place in the zero to 3 km layer.  LCL values were in the 600 to 1000 meter range.  There was plenty of CAPE available in the -10 to -30 C. layer with 500 to 700 J/kg available.  In spite of this, little hail occurred with the freezing level quite high, nearly 15,000 feet.   Downdraft CAPE was around 1500 J/kg, favoring winds.  Thunderstorms erupted north of the warm front.  They formed in two areas. Scattered thunderstorms developed across north central Iowa along an elevated convergence zone in a near tropical atmosphere.  Feed by a low level jet of 30 knots (about 35 mph), the thunderstorms evolved into a squall line that raced east southeast across eastern Iowa and northern Illinois.  Wind damage was the most common severe report with severe crop damage occurring across a large portion of Benton County, and to a lesser extent across Iowa, Linn, and Clinton Counties.  The wind combined with a large amount of cloud to ground lightning resulted in power failures across parts of eastern Iowa and took two public safety communication towers off the air.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: measured 46 knot (53 mph) gust at 1532 CST along with pea size hail

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying MCS interacted with a semi-moist to moist atmosphere to produce a widespread general rain across eastern Iowa.  Thunderstorms that developed during the day inside the MCS produced intense localized rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: from co-op observer; 3.01" inches of rain in 1 hour; most fell between 1200 to 1240 CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying MCS interacted with a semi-moist to moist atmosphere to produce a widespread general rain across eastern Iowa.  Thunderstorms that developed during the day inside the MCS produced intense localized rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized street flooding at Locust & Iowa streets; water over the curbs in eastbound lanes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance initiated broken lines of thunderstorms in a near tropical atmosphere across eastern Iowa.  A few of the storms pulsed severe with severe hail and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: small to medium branches down near Bowman Park in NE Cedar Rapids; also a large tree reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved southeast across the area during the previous 12 hours.  The airmass it replaced was very warm and humid with dew points in the mid 50s and temperatures in the 60s.  Behind the front, temperatures fell rapidly into the teens and 20s.  A persistent southwest flow aloft drew warm and moist air over the top of the cold surface layer.  This resulted in a mix of precipitation with rain changing to freezing rain, then eventually to snow.  There were numerous reports of ice accumulating to 1/4 to 1/2 inch thickness.  The accumulation was mainly on trees and elevated surfaces.  Road conditions did not become too bad as the ground temperatures were quiet warm.

A cold front dropped southeast through Iowa during the day on the 9th and became stationary south of the state.  During the night of the 9th into the early morning of the 10th, low pressure formed over Oklahoma.  The low lifted northeast along the front during the day on the 10th.  A large area of over running precipitation developed.  The airmass became very unstable with a southerly jet of 35 to 40 kts lifting over the cooler air at the surface.  Dew points at the 850 mb level were near +10 C. in the warm air.  Considerable destabilization took place with elevated CAPE rising into the 3000 to 4000 J/kg range over the east half of Iowa.  The freezing level was around 10,000 feet, while the available cape in the -10 to -30 C. layer was around 200 J/kg.  Thunderstorms erupted over a large part of Iowa, with a line of stronger storms developing in the south central into parts of central Iowa.  Thunderstorms produced nickel size hail in Jasper County, which covered the ground.  Penny size hail covered the ground in Poweshiek County from another severe storm.  To the northwest, snow, mixed with freezing rain and sleet at times, was the problem.  The snow began accumulating around 0400 CST, with the heavier snow beginning around 0600 CST.  Heavy snow fell over the northern two tiers of counties, with 6 to 10 inches common.  The heaviest snow fell in a band from Palo Alto County, across Kossuth County, into Winnebago County with 8 to 10 inches common.  In Cerro Gordo County, snowfall was lighter with 3 to 4 inches common.  In addition to the snow, heavy sleet fell at the onset of of the storm.  Reports of around an inch of sleet were received.  The snowfall tapered off rapidly too the south of the heavy snow area with amounts south of U.S. Highway 20, south to Interstate 80, generally in the 1 to 3 inch range.

Freezing rain the evening of November 26th through the morning of November 27th caused light ice accumulations of a tenth of an inch or less. The freezing rain made roads and other surfaces icy, resulting in numerous traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain caused light ice accumulations, but made roads and other surfaces icy. There were numerous accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure moved out of the southern Plains into Iowa during the afternoon of the 30th into the 31st.  There was very little in the way of cold air associated with the system and much of the precipitation it produced was in the form rain.  As the upper low pushed into Iowa on the morning of 31st, the rain began to change over to snow.  There was a brief period of freezing rain during the changeover, however the freezing rain was not a major factor.  Snow fell on the northwest edge of the system through much of the day.  A narrow band of 6 inch snowfall was reported from the Estherville area, east to Swea City, and southwest into Crawford County near Schleswig.

An upper level low pressure system tracked across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa and brought several reports of dime to nickel size hail to southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.

A surface low pressure tracked east across Iowa during the early morning hours of the 4th.  A frontal boundary extended northeast to southwest through the low and separated very warm and moist air from seasonal cooler air to the north.  South of the front, temperatures remained in the upper 70s to low 80s through the night with dew points in the upper 50s to mid 60s.  The overall atmosphere was relatively dry with the soundings exhibiting an inverted V structure.  The airmass was capped through the afternoon of the 3rd into the early morning of the 4th with 700 mb temperatures in the 8 to 11 C. range.  An upper level short wave dropped southeast into the area, weakening the the cap.  Thunderstorms developed after midnight in southern Minnesota and dropped east-southeast into northern Iowa.  The storms were elevated in nature with 2500 J/kg of CAPE available in the elevated layer.  Lifted indices were marginal, in the zero to -2 C. range.  There was plenty of shear available with effective shear values of 55 kts.  The freezing level was quite high for October, at 13,000 feet, however the CAPE in the -10 to -30 C. layer of the sounding in the 300 to 500 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms dropped southeast into Iowa and produced large hail in Worth County.  Hail up to the size of baseballs was reported southeast of Northwood.  The hail was accompanied by winds of 60 to 70 MPH as well.  Baseball size hail also fell east-northeast of Scarville.  The hail broke out all of the windows on the west side of a house there.  In some areas, excessive amounts of hail caused considerable crop damage to the soybean crops in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow, accumulating to 3 to 6 inches, fell on the night ov November 9th into the morning of November 10th. The accumulations were reported in northwest Iowa from Spiirit Lake and Spencer to Storm Lake and Ida Grove, with little or no snow further west near the western border of Iowa. The snowfall was not accompanied by significant winds and caused fairly minimal traffic disruptions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 3 to 6 inches caused minor travel disruptions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain which spread into northwest Iowa Saturday December 30th changed to snow during the morning of December 31st. The snow accumulated 4 to 8 inches in the area from Spencer to Storm Lake, with the greatest accumulations about halfway between these locations on the Clay/Buena Vista county line. Moderately strong northwest winds caused drifting and some blowing snow, especially while the snow continued to fall. Travel was greatly slowed over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow on the morning of December 31st. The snow accumulated 4 to 8 inches, with the heaviest accumulation near the northern edge of Buena Vista County. The snow decreased by late afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved southeast across the area during the previous 12 hours.  The airmass it replaced was very warm and humid with dew points in the mid 50s and temperatures in the 60s.  Behind the front, temperatures fell rapidly into the teens and 20s.  A persistent southwest flow aloft drew warm and moist air over the top of the cold surface layer.  This resulted in a mix of precipitation with rain changing to freezing rain, then eventually to snow.  There were numerous reports of ice accumulating to 1/4 to 1/2 inch thickness.  The accumulation was mainly on trees and elevated surfaces.  Road conditions did not become too bad as the ground temperatures were quiet warm.

A major winter storm, the first significant winter event of the season, impacted an area roughly along and east of the Mississippi River. Strong surface low pressure travelled from Arkansas to Lake Huron, while the associated upper level low moved from Oklahoma to northern Illinois. Copious amounts of gulf moisture fed into the system, while very cold and dry air filtered into eastern Iowa. This caused a sharp cut-off to the snow, with 20-30 miles separating 10 inches of snow from little to no snow. At least 6 to 10 inches of snow were common along and east of the Mississippi River, with the highest amount of 18 inches measured at Princeton, Illinois in Bureau county. A 70 mile stretch of I-80 was closed for many hours in Illinois due to accidents and deep snow, with some people stuck in traffic for up to 8 hours. To add to the nearly impossible driving conditions, freezing rain and sleet preceded the heavy snow. The Illinois DNR delivered food by snowmobile to people stuck in traffic or stranded at rest stops. The Illinois governor declared 49 counties state disaster areas, including Bureau and Putnam counties in the DVN CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of snow fell in the extreme eastern part of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a sharp cut-off to the snow in this county. Five to over 8 inches of snow fell in the eastern part of the county. Near Le Claire, 8.5 inches of snow was measured. Meanwhile, the western part of the county had little or no snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from southern Kansas during the late afternoon, into southwest Iowa during the late evening hours.  A warm front lifted lifted north into Iowa ahead of the low, ushering in dew points in the mid to upper 40s.  Thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the warm front with a large area of elevated convection forming over Iowa.  CAPE values were in the 500 to 700 J/kg range in the most unstable layer.  Most of the thunderstorms produced pea to marble size hail and many areas reported the ground covered by hail.  A few of the storms produced some marginal severe weather with three quarter inch diamter hail reported over parts of central Iowa as they passed.  The largest hail occurred in Adams County, where nickel size hail fell south of Prescott.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure tracked east across Iowa during the early morning hours of the 4th.  A frontal boundary extended northeast to southwest through the low and separated very warm and moist air from seasonal cooler air to the north.  South of the front, temperatures remained in the upper 70s to low 80s through the night with dew points in the upper 50s to mid 60s.  The overall atmosphere was relatively dry with the soundings exhibiting an inverted V structure.  The airmass was capped through the afternoon of the 3rd into the early morning of the 4th with 700 mb temperatures in the 8 to 11 C. range.  An upper level short wave dropped southeast into the area, weakening the the cap.  Thunderstorms developed after midnight in southern Minnesota and dropped east-southeast into northern Iowa.  The storms were elevated in nature with 2500 J/kg of CAPE available in the elevated layer.  Lifted indices were marginal, in the zero to -2 C. range.  There was plenty of shear available with effective shear values of 55 kts.  The freezing level was quite high for October, at 13,000 feet, however the CAPE in the -10 to -30 C. layer of the sounding in the 300 to 500 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms dropped southeast into Iowa and produced large hail in Worth County.  Hail up to the size of baseballs was reported southeast of Northwood.  The hail was accompanied by winds of 60 to 70 MPH as well.  Baseball size hail also fell east-northeast of Scarville.  The hail broke out all of the windows on the west side of a house there.  In some areas, excessive amounts of hail caused considerable crop damage to the soybean crops in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the southern Plains to near Quincy, Illinois and then into southern lower Michigan. A cold front moving through eastern Iowa and western Illinois triggered thunderstorms during the late morning and afternoon hours. The storms formed in a moist and weak to moderately unstable airmass above a stable layer, as the cold front was rather shallow initially. This type of environment favored hail, and there were many reports of pea size hail covering the ground, as surface temperatures were only in the 30s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inch tree limb down in a hospital parking lot. Also pea size hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped southeast through Iowa during the day on the 9th and became stationary south of the state.  During the night of the 9th into the early morning of the 10th, low pressure formed over Oklahoma.  The low lifted northeast along the front during the day on the 10th.  A large area of over running precipitation developed.  The airmass became very unstable with a southerly jet of 35 to 40 kts lifting over the cooler air at the surface.  Dew points at the 850 mb level were near +10 C. in the warm air.  Considerable destabilization took place with elevated CAPE rising into the 3000 to 4000 J/kg range over the east half of Iowa.  The freezing level was around 10,000 feet, while the available cape in the -10 to -30 C. layer was around 200 J/kg.  Thunderstorms erupted over a large part of Iowa, with a line of stronger storms developing in the south central into parts of central Iowa.  Thunderstorms produced nickel size hail in Jasper County, which covered the ground.  Penny size hail covered the ground in Poweshiek County from another severe storm.  To the northwest, snow, mixed with freezing rain and sleet at times, was the problem.  The snow began accumulating around 0400 CST, with the heavier snow beginning around 0600 CST.  Heavy snow fell over the northern two tiers of counties, with 6 to 10 inches common.  The heaviest snow fell in a band from Palo Alto County, across Kossuth County, into Winnebago County with 8 to 10 inches common.  In Cerro Gordo County, snowfall was lighter with 3 to 4 inches common.  In addition to the snow, heavy sleet fell at the onset of of the storm.  Reports of around an inch of sleet were received.  The snowfall tapered off rapidly too the south of the heavy snow area with amounts south of U.S. Highway 20, south to Interstate 80, generally in the 1 to 3 inch range.

A supercell moved across northeast Iowa during the early morning hours of October 4, producing large hail up to the size of baseballs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 12 mile swath of hail damage was observed on both sides of Indigo Road and Highway 9 in Mitchell County. Corncobs were stipped off stalks and soybean fields were shredded. Hail drifts were still two feet deep at 9:15 a.m. from hail that fell at 3:55 a.m.  In addition, numerous vehicles in the area were damaged along with structures that had windows broken out.

The freezing level was around 10,000 feet, while the available cape in the -10 to -30 C. layer was around 200 J/kg.  Thunderstorms erupted over a large part of Iowa, with a line of stronger storms developing in the south central into parts of central Iowa.  Thunderstorms produced nickel size hail in Jasper County, which covered the ground.  Penny size hail covered the ground in Poweshiek County from another severe storm.  To the northwest, snow, mixed with freezing rain and sleet at times, was the problem.  The snow began accumulating around 0400 CST, with the heavier snow beginning around 0600 CST.  Heavy snow fell over the northern two tiers of counties, with 6 to 10 inches common.  The heaviest snow fell in a band from Palo Alto County, across Kossuth County, into Winnebago County with 8 to 10 inches common.  In Cerro Gordo County, snowfall was lighter with 3 to 4 inches common.  In addition to the snow, heavy sleet fell at the onset of of the storm.  Reports of around an inch of sleet were received.  The snowfall tapered off rapidly too the south of the heavy snow area with amounts south of U.S. Highway 20, south to Interstate 80, generally in the 1 to 3 inch range.
